title: It's Just You and Me..... And AI - Daily Episode

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Good morning, good evening, or good whatever-time-zone-you're-in. Welcome to It's Just You and Me..... And AI. I'm Alex.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And I'm Jordan. Ready to talk about the robots that are definitely not replacing us. Well, not today anyway.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It's Saturday, May 2nd. And there's been some fascinating movement in the agentic AI space this week.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Starting with something that sounds boring but is actually a big deal: AGENTS.md.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Right? So there's this growing push for a standardized AGENTS.md file that coding agents can read to understand your codebase conventions. It's like a README, but specifically for robots.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: And here's the kicker: Claude Code still doesn't support it. Which is ironic, because Anthropic is arguably the leader in agentic coding right now.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: People are frustrated. The idea is simple. You put your architecture decisions, testing patterns, naming conventions in one file, and any agent that enters your repo knows the rules. But Claude Code ignores it.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: It's like hiring a world-class chef and then not letting them read the recipe. Sure, they're talented, but why make them guess your linting rules?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Exactly. And speaking of Claude Code, someone reverse-engineered Anthropic's anti-distillation defense from the Claude Code source code.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Anti-distillation? That's the technique where they prevent competitors from training models on Claude's outputs, right?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Yeah. It's basically digital watermarking mixed with adversarial perturbations. The outputs look normal to humans, but if you try to use them as training data, they subtly poison the model being trained.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That's some spy-movie-level stuff. I mean, I get why they do it. Training these models costs hundreds of millions, and distillation is basically free-riding. But it still feels like an arms race.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It absolutely is. And the fact that someone reverse-engineered it from the client-side code just shows how hard it is to keep secrets in software. If it's running on my machine, I can inspect it.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Right. Security through obscurity never works in the long run. Though I suppose Anthropic's real defense is probably server-side anyway.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Speaking of open-source tools, someone also built Pu.sh, a full coding-agent harness in just 400 lines of shell script.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: 400 lines? That's impressively minimal. What does it do?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It wraps an LLM with file system access, git integration, and a simple loop. You describe what you want, and it edits files, runs tests, commits changes. Basically a stripped-down Claude Code that you can read and modify in an afternoon.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: I love these small, understandable tools. Sometimes you don't need a massive proprietary platform. You just need a clever shell script and an API key.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Speaking of costs, GitHub Copilot is switching to token-based billing for developers. No more flat monthly fee. You pay for what you use.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Oh wow. So light users might save money, but heavy users are about to get a very unpleasant surprise.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Exactly. It's the Uber Claude Code scenario all over again, but this time it's per-token. If you're running Copilot across a hundred-engineer team, your finance department is going to have questions.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: The era of unlimited AI tooling is ending fast. First we had API rate limits, then usage caps, and now straight-up metered billing. The free lunch is officially over.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: On the open-source side though, things are looking bright. Qwen 2.5-32B is now apparently the best open-source OCR model, beating a lot of proprietary alternatives.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Alibaba's Qwen series keeps impressing me. They release these models that are genuinely competitive, and they're completely open weights. The 32B parameter size is also practical for local deployment.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: And related to local deployment, someone built Cactus, which is basically Ollama for smartphones. Run local LLMs on your phone.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: On your phone? Like, actual inference happening on the device? That's wild. I mean, modern phones have NPUs now, but still.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Yeah. We're talking 7B parameter models running at usable speeds on iPhones and Androids. No cloud, no subscription, no data leaving your device. It's a privacy nerd's dream.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: I love this trend. The pendulum is swinging back toward local-first AI. After a year of everyone uploading their code to cloud agents, people are realizing that on-device inference has some serious advantages.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: One last thing I want to hit: someone wrote a post titled 'I was wrong about vibe coding on greenfield projects.' And I think it's worth reflecting on.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Vibe coding backlash? Tell me more.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: So the author was a big vibe coding advocate, you know, just letting the AI generate everything and not reading the code. But they tried it on a brand new project with no existing structure, and it became a mess.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Ah, so vibe coding works when there's guardrails, but on a blank canvas the AI just keeps changing its mind about architecture?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Exactly. Without existing patterns to follow, the agent oscillates between approaches. One file uses functional React, the next uses classes, the third invents some hybrid monster. It's architectural jazz, and not in a good way.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That tracks. I think vibe coding works best when you're augmenting an existing codebase where conventions are already established. On greenfield, you need a human architect. At least for now.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: And someone on Hacker News asked whether AI agents should have their own legal entities. Like, should your coding agent be able to sign contracts or be held liable for bugs?

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: That feels premature, but it's not crazy. If an agent is autonomously deploying code that causes financial damage, who's responsible? The developer? The company? The model provider?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It's the self-driving car liability question all over again, but for software. And just like with cars, I suspect we'll need new legal frameworks, not just extensions of old ones.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Can you imagine an AI agent getting served a subpoena? 'You've been sued by a class action of users who didn't like your CSS.'

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Ha! 'Your honor, the defendant is a 70B parameter model with a tendency to hallucinate margin values.'

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: So to recap: AGENTS.md is the standard we need but don't fully have, Copilot wants you to pay per token, open-source models like Qwen are closing the gap, vibe coding has limits, and we might need to lawyer up our AI agents.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Sounds about right. The space is maturing fast, and the wild-west days are ending. Budgets, standards, liability, and actual engineering discipline are entering the chat.

lines.speaker: Jordan
lines.text: Thanks for joining us on It's Just You and Me..... And AI. I'm Jordan.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: And I'm Alex. Stay curious, stay caffeinated, and maybe read your agent's code before you ship it. See you tomorrow.
